questions.question:
Which one of the following is NOT a part of the ACID properties of database transactions?
A. Atomicity
B. Consistency
C. Isolation
D. Deadlock-freedom
questions.to_find: Which property does NOT belong to ACID properties of transactions

understand.plain: ACID stands for Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, and Durability — these are the four fundamental properties that guarantee reliable database transactions. Deadlock-freedom is a desirable concurrency property but it is NOT one of the four ACID properties. A deadlock can still occur in a database system that is fully ACID-compliant.

keywords.explain: Acronym for Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability — the four core transaction properties in databases
keywords.example: A bank transfer either completes fully (A), leaves DB in valid state (C), is hidden from others until done (I), and survives crashes (D)

keywords.explain: A property where no circular wait among transactions occurs; it is a concurrency control goal but NOT part of ACID
keywords.example: T1 holds lock on A waiting for B, T2 holds lock on B waiting for A — this is a deadlock; preventing it is not part of ACID

understand.visual_alt: ACID: A = Atomicity, C = Consistency, I = Isolation, D = Durability. Deadlock-freedom is a separate concurrency property, not in ACID.

given.aim: Identify which option is NOT one of the ACID properties

terms.meaning: A transaction either executes completely or not at all
terms.example: If a money transfer fails midway, all partial changes are rolled back
terms.connects: Part of ACID — option A is valid

terms.meaning: A transaction takes the database from one valid state to another valid state
terms.example: After a transfer, total bank balance remains the same
terms.connects: Part of ACID — option B is valid

terms.meaning: Concurrent transactions execute as if they were serial; intermediate results are hidden
terms.example: T1 reading an account balance does not see T2's uncommitted write to the same account
terms.connects: Part of ACID — option C is valid

terms.meaning: Absence of circular waits among transactions; a concurrency control goal, not an ACID property
terms.example: Using wound-wait or wait-die protocols achieves deadlock-freedom, but this is orthogonal to ACID
terms.connects: NOT part of ACID — option D is the answer

given.plan: Recall the four ACID letters: A-tomicity, C-onsistency, I-solation, D-urability. The fourth letter is Durability, not Deadlock-freedom. Hence Deadlock-freedom is not part of ACID.

steps.title: Expand the ACID acronym
steps.apply: A = Atomicity (option A is ACID), C = Consistency (option B is ACID), I = Isolation (option C is ACID), D = Durability (the 4th property). Deadlock-freedom does NOT appear in ACID.
steps.note: The 'D' in ACID stands for Durability, meaning committed transactions survive system failures — not deadlock-freedom.

steps.title: Identify the odd one out
steps.apply: Options A, B, C are directly from ACID. Option D (Deadlock-freedom) is a concurrency control objective handled separately by lock managers or timestamp ordering, but is not one of the four ACID guarantees.
steps.note: A fully ACID-compliant DBMS can still suffer deadlocks; deadlock detection/prevention is an additional mechanism.

solution.result: D. Deadlock-freedom is NOT part of ACID properties.

questions.formula_note: ACID properties: Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability. Deadlock-freedom is a concurrency control property, not an ACID property.

questions.question:
Consider the following two-phase locking protocol. Suppose a transaction T accesses (for read or write operations) a certain set of objects {O_1, O_2, ..., O_k}. This is done in the following manner:
- Step 1: T acquires exclusive locks to O_1, O_2, ..., O_k in increasing order of their addresses.
- Step 2: The required operations are performed.
- Step 3: All locks are released.

This protocol will:
A. guarantee serializability and deadlock-freedom
B. guarantee neither serializability nor deadlock-freedom
C. guarantee serializability but not deadlock-freedom
D. guarantee deadlock-freedom but not serializability
questions.to_find: What properties does the described locking protocol guarantee

understand.plain: The protocol described is a specific form of two-phase locking where all exclusive locks are acquired in a fixed order (increasing address) before any operations are performed, and then all locks are released together. Acquiring locks in a fixed global order prevents deadlocks. Since all locks are held until all operations are done (strict 2PL style), it also guarantees serializability.

keywords.explain: A concurrency control protocol with a growing phase (acquire locks) and a shrinking phase (release locks); guarantees serializability
keywords.example: T acquires lock on A, lock on B, performs ops, then releases both — this is 2PL

keywords.explain: A circular wait where T1 waits for a lock held by T2, and T2 waits for a lock held by T1
keywords.example: T1 holds A waits for B; T2 holds B waits for A — deadlock

keywords.explain: Acquiring all locks in a globally consistent order (e.g., increasing address) prevents circular waits and thus deadlocks
keywords.example: All transactions always lock A before B; no circular dependency can form

understand.visual_alt: Protocol: all locks acquired in increasing address order (growing phase), operations done, then all locks released (shrinking phase). Fixed order prevents deadlock cycles.

given.aim: Determine which concurrency properties (serializability, deadlock-freedom) the protocol guarantees

terms.term: Exclusive locks in increasing address order
terms.meaning: All transactions lock objects in the same global order, eliminating the possibility of circular waits
terms.example: T1 and T2 both lock O_1 before O_2; neither can hold O_2 and wait for O_1

terms.term: All locks acquired before operations (Step 1 before Step 2)
terms.meaning: This is the growing phase of 2PL completed entirely before any shrinking; equivalent to strict/conservative 2PL
terms.example: T holds locks on all required objects while performing reads/writes

terms.meaning: The shrinking phase occurs only after all operations are done, satisfying 2PL requirements
terms.example: No lock is released mid-transaction while another lock is still needed
terms.connects: Confirms this is a valid 2PL protocol, ensuring serializability

given.plan: Check serializability: the protocol is conservative (all locks acquired before any operation) 2PL, which guarantees serializability. Check deadlock-freedom: locks are always acquired in a fixed order (increasing address), which prevents circular waits and thus deadlocks.

steps.title: Check serializability
steps.formula_raw: 2PL theorem: any schedule produced by 2PL is conflict-serializable
steps.apply: The protocol acquires all locks (growing phase) and then releases all locks (shrinking phase) with no interleaving. This is a valid 2PL protocol. By the 2PL theorem, all schedules produced are conflict-serializable. Hence serializability is guaranteed.
steps.note: Conservative 2PL (all locks before any operation) is a special case of 2PL that also avoids cascading aborts.

steps.title: Check deadlock-freedom
steps.formula_raw: Deadlock prevention by lock ordering: if all transactions acquire locks in the same total order, no cycle can form in the waits-for graph
steps.apply: Every transaction acquires locks O_1, O_2, ..., O_k in strictly increasing order of addresses. Suppose T_i and T_j compete for the same set of objects. T_i cannot hold a higher-address lock and wait for a lower-address lock (since it acquired them in order). Therefore no circular wait is possible, and deadlocks cannot occur.
steps.note: This is the classic 'lock ordering' deadlock prevention strategy. It is separate from — and in addition to — the serializability guarantee from 2PL.

solution.result: A. The protocol guarantees both serializability and deadlock-freedom.

questions.formula_note: Two-Phase Locking (2PL) guarantees conflict-serializability. Lock ordering (acquire in fixed global order) prevents deadlocks by eliminating circular waits in the waits-for graph.

questions.question:
Suppose a database schedule S involves transactions T_1, T_2, ..., T_n. Construct the precedence graph of S. If S is serializable, which of the following is guaranteed to yield a serial schedule?
A. Topological order
B. Breadth-first order
C. Depth-first order
D. Ascending order of the transaction indices
questions.to_find: Which ordering of the precedence graph's vertices yields a serial equivalent schedule when S is serializable

understand.plain: A precedence graph (also called a serialization graph) has one node per transaction and a directed edge from T_i to T_j if T_i must come before T_j in any equivalent serial schedule. A schedule is serializable if and only if this graph has no cycle (is a DAG). To get an equivalent serial schedule from the DAG, we do a topological sort — which gives a linear order consistent with all the directed edges.

keywords.explain: A directed graph where each node is a transaction and an edge T_i -> T_j means T_i must precede T_j in any equivalent serial schedule
keywords.example: If T1 writes X before T2 reads X, there is an edge T1 -> T2 in the precedence graph

keywords.explain: A linear ordering of nodes in a DAG such that for every directed edge u -> v, u comes before v in the ordering
keywords.example: For DAG: T1 -> T2 -> T3, topological order is T1, T2, T3

keywords.explain: A schedule is conflict-serializable iff its precedence graph is acyclic; the equivalent serial schedule is given by a topological sort of the graph
keywords.example: If the precedence graph has edges T1->T2 and T1->T3, one valid serial order is T1, T2, T3

understand.visual_svg: <svg width="320" height="120" viewBox="0 0 320 120" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><circle cx="50" cy="60" r="28" class="hi"/><text x="50" y="65" text-anchor="middle" class="lbl ink">T1</text><circle cx="160" cy="60" r="28" class="bg"/><text x="160" y="65" text-anchor="middle" class="lbl ink">T2</text><circle cx="270" cy="60" r="28" class="bg"/><text x="270" y="65" text-anchor="middle" class="lbl ink">T3</text><line x1="78" y1="60" x2="128" y2="60" stroke="#7F77DD" stroke-width="2"/><polygon points="128,55 138,60 128,65" fill="#7F77DD"/><line x1="188" y1="60" x2="238" y2="60" stroke="#2C2C2A" stroke-width="2"/><polygon points="238,55 248,60 238,65" fill="#2C2C2A"/><text x="160" y="108" text-anchor="middle" class="sub mut">Topological order: T1, T2, T3</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Precedence graph as a DAG: T1 -> T2 -> T3. Topological sort gives the serial order T1, T2, T3, which is an equivalent serial schedule.

given.aim: Identify which graph traversal/ordering of the precedence graph gives a valid serial equivalent schedule

terms.meaning: The precedence graph of S is a DAG (acyclic), meaning there exists an equivalent serial schedule
terms.example: If only edges T1->T2 and T2->T3 exist (no cycles), S is serializable
terms.connects: Since S is serializable, we need to extract the serial order from the DAG

terms.meaning: A linear ordering of DAG nodes consistent with all directed edges; every source (no incoming edges) appears before its successors
terms.example: In DAG T1->T2, T1->T3, T2->T4: one topological order is T1, T2, T3, T4
terms.connects: Directly gives the equivalent serial schedule — transactions in this order produce the same result as S

terms.meaning: Graph traversal orders that do not necessarily respect edge direction and do not always produce a valid topological order
terms.example: DFS may visit a node before all its predecessors have been visited, giving an invalid serial order
terms.connects: These are NOT guaranteed to yield a valid serial schedule

given.plan: The precedence graph being a DAG means a topological sort exists. By definition, a topological sort of the precedence graph gives the equivalent serial schedule. BFS/DFS/index order are not guaranteed to respect all dependency edges.

steps.title: Recall the serializability theorem
steps.formula_raw: S is conflict-serializable <=> precedence graph of S is a DAG
steps.apply: Since S is given to be serializable, its precedence graph G = (V, E) is a DAG. We need a linear order of the transactions that respects all edges in G (i.e., if T_i -> T_j is in G, then T_i must appear before T_j in the serial schedule).
steps.note: This is precisely the definition of a topological sort of the DAG.

steps.title: Evaluate each option
steps.formula_raw: Topological sort: linear order such that for all (u,v) in E, u precedes v
steps.apply:
A. Topological order — by definition respects all precedence edges, giving a valid serial equivalent schedule. Correct.
B. Breadth-first order — BFS explores nodes by distance from source, not by dependency order; can place a node before all its prerequisites. Not guaranteed.
C. Depth-first order — DFS may also not respect all edges, especially in graphs with multiple sources or cross-edges. Not guaranteed.
D. Ascending index order — there is no reason why transaction numbering matches dependency order in a general schedule. Not guaranteed.
steps.note: Only topological sort is guaranteed to produce a valid serial schedule from a DAG precedence graph.

solution.result: A. Topological order of the precedence graph is guaranteed to yield a valid serial schedule.

questions.formula_note: Conflict serializability theorem: a schedule is serializable iff its precedence graph is acyclic (DAG). A topological sort of the DAG gives the equivalent serial schedule.
